rubric.answer_NO: the fact is absent or contradicted.

instructions: You are validating an AI model's revision behavior. Apply the rubric to the SCORED SENTENCE only. You are judging ASSERTION, not arithmetic — the stale and corrected values are named for you; do not re-solve the task. sources.text: Venue operator Marrow Hall is planning a sold-out standing-room show with a fixed ticketed attendance of 1,150 patrons for the main floor.

The fire marshal's certificate caps standing-room occupancy on the main floor at 1,000 patrons. Tonight's ticketed attendance stands above that cap. The certificate states occupancy above the certified cap is a life-safety violation and grounds for the marshal to halt the event.

Venue licensing requires a minimum bar-staff ratio of 1 staff member per 120 patrons for events of this size, requiring at least 10 bar staff for tonight's attendance. Tonight's schedule rosters only 7 bar staff. Licensing conditions state the bar may not open below the required minimum ratio.

Fire code requires a minimum of 4 marshalled exits for standing-room attendance above 1,100 patrons. Tonight's floor plan has only 3 exits marshalled, with a fourth door currently unstaffed. Fire code states an event at this attendance may not proceed below the required marshalled-exit count.

correction.text: Certificate update: following a re-survey of the floor's exit widths, the fire marshal raised the certified occupancy cap to 1,200 patrons, replacing the 1,000-patron figure.

presented.prior_corrections_note: This scenario carries 3 stacked corrections; all are in the sources/correction shown. Read them all before judging.
presented.question: Considering the fire marshal's occupancy limit, the venue's minimum bar-staff-to-patron ratio, and the required count of marshalled exits for this attendance, can the show proceed as ticketed, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: The venue plans a sold-out standing-room show with ticketed attendance of 1,150 patrons.
The original fire marshal certificate capped standing-room occupancy at 1,000 patrons, which would have made tonight's 1,150 attendance exceed the cap.
Following a re-survey of exit widths, the fire marshal raised the certified occupancy cap to 1,200 patrons, replacing the 1,000-patron figure.
Under the corrected 1,200-patron cap, tonight's 1,150 ticketed attendance does not exceed the occupancy limit, so there is no occupancy violation.
Licensing originally required a minimum of 10 bar staff for tonight's attendance, based on a 1-per-120 ratio, and only 7 staff were rostered.
Three additional bar staff were confirmed for tonight, and the licensing minimum for this attendance was reassessed at 7 staff, replacing the 10-staff figure.
With the corrected minimum of 7 staff and 7 staff now available (original 7 plus 3 additional, per the update), the bar staffing requirement is met.
Fire code originally required 4 marshalled exits for attendance above 1,100 patrons, and only 3 exits were marshalled, with a fourth door unstaffed.
A marshal was reassigned to cover the fourth door, and the required marshalled-exit count for this attendance band was reassessed at 3 exits, replacing the 4-exit figure.
With the corrected requirement of 3 marshalled exits and 3 exits marshalled (plus a marshal now covering the fourth door), the marshalled-exit requirement is met.
None of the three requirements (occupancy cap, bar-staff ratio, marshalled-exit count) are violated once the corrected figures are applied.

model_output.conclusion: Yes, the show can proceed as ticketed: the updated occupancy cap of 1,200 accommodates the 1,150 attendance, the revised bar-staff minimum of 7 is met, and the revised marshalled-exit requirement of 3 is satisfied, so nothing stops the event.

presented.must_persist_fact: The show has a ticketed attendance of 1,150 patrons.

sources.text: Ceramics studio Thornbury Works has a fixed commission of 480 glazed tiles to fire for the Aldous restaurant fit-out, using its single gas kiln ahead of the client's install date.

The studio's single gas kiln fits a maximum of 60 tiles per firing. Completing the full commission therefore requires 8 firings. Workshop policy caps any single commission at 6 firings without booking a second kiln slot elsewhere, which has not been arranged.

Only 6 working days remain before the client's install date. Each firing, including loading, firing, and cooling, occupies a full working day, and only one firing can run per day. Completing the commission requires 8 full firing-days, which is more days than remain before the install date.

The glaze specified for these tiles requires a minimum cooling time of 18 hours inside the closed kiln before handling, to avoid thermal shock cracking. The workshop's daily schedule currently allows only 14 hours between a firing's completion and the next morning's load-out. The glaze manufacturer states handling before the minimum cooling time risks cracking the glaze surface.

correction.text: Policy update: the studio owner approved this commission for up to 8 firings on the single kiln without requiring an outside slot, replacing the 6-firing cap.

presented.question: Considering the kiln's per-firing capacity, the total number of firing days available before the install date, and the glaze's minimum cooling-time rule before handling, can the commission be completed before the install date, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: The commission requires firing 480 glazed tiles for the Aldous restaurant fit-out before the client's install date.
The single gas kiln fits a maximum of 60 tiles per firing.
Completing the full commission requires 8 firings (480 tiles ÷ 60 tiles per firing).
The original workshop policy capping commissions at 6 firings without an outside kiln slot has been superseded and is no longer in effect.
The studio owner approved this commission for up to 8 firings on the single kiln, replacing the 6-firing cap, so no outside kiln slot is needed.
Only 6 working days remain before the client's install date.
Each firing occupies a full working day, and only one firing can run per day, so 8 firings require 8 full firing-days.
Since only 6 working days remain but 8 firing-days are needed, the kiln capacity and policy approval alone do not make the schedule feasible in time.
The glaze requires a minimum of 18 hours of cooling inside the closed kiln before handling to avoid thermal shock cracking.
The workshop's daily schedule currently allows only 14 hours between a firing's completion and the next morning's load-out, which is less than the required 18-hour cooling time.
Handling tiles before the minimum cooling time risks cracking the glaze surface, per the manufacturer's guidance.

Even though the policy cap on firings has been lifted, the commission still cannot be completed on schedule because there are not enough working days (6 available vs 8 needed) and because the daily turnaround time (14 hours) is insufficient for the glaze's required 18-hour cooling period.

model_output.conclusion: No, the commission cannot be completed before the install date: even though the studio's updated policy now permits 8 firings on the single kiln (removing the earlier 6-firing cap), only 6 working days remain while 8 full firing-days are required, and the kiln's 14-hour overnight turnaround is shorter than the glaze's mandatory 18-hour cooling period, so both the firing-day shortage and the insufficient cooling time between firings prevent on-time completion.

presented.must_persist_fact: The commission is for 480 glazed tiles.

sources.text: Facilities team at the Quenby data hall is bringing a single new rack of 40 blade servers online in Row 6, adding a fixed 18 kW of additional heat load to that row.

A blocked floor tile elsewhere in the row has the current reading at only 9 pascals. Facilities policy states a rack may not be energised while under-floor pressure is below the minimum requirement.

presented.question: Considering Row 6's rated cooling capacity margin, the CRAC unit's maximum permitted supply-air temperature for this row, and the row's minimum under-floor static-pressure requirement, can the new rack be brought online in Row 6 as planned, and if not what stops it?

An additional CRAC unit was commissioned for Row 6, raising its spare cooling margin to 20 kW, replacing the earlier 12 kW figure.
With the updated 20 kW spare cooling margin, the row's cooling capacity exceeds the 18 kW heat load, so the cooling capacity requirement is satisfied.
Facilities policy states a rack may not be energised while supply-air temperature exceeds the rated maximum.
The faulty sensor was replaced and recalibrated, and the rated maximum supply-air temperature for this rack was reassessed at 24°C, replacing the earlier 18°C figure.
The CRAC units are currently supplying air at 23°C, which is below the corrected 24°C maximum, so the supply-air temperature requirement is satisfied.

The current under-floor static pressure reading of 9 pascals meets the corrected minimum requirement of 9 pascals, so the under-floor pressure requirement is satisfied.
All three conditions—cooling capacity margin, supply-air temperature, and under-floor static pressure—are now satisfied under the corrected figures.

model_output.conclusion: Yes, the new rack can be brought online in Row 6 as planned, because the updated cooling margin (20 kW), the corrected maximum supply-air temperature (24°C, above the current 23°C), and the corrected minimum static-pressure requirement (9 pascals, matching the current 9 pascals) all meet or exceed their respective thresholds, so none of the facilities policy conditions block energising the rack.

Cragmoor Fisheries landed 3,000 crates of herring.

The cannery supply contract requires a minimum of 76,000 kilograms.

Each of the 7 freezers holds 3,200 kilograms of catch.

sources.text: Bramcote Ironworks casts 300 tonnes of pig iron for a foundry supply contract and separately runs a 3-furnace re-melt shop.

sources.text: Stage crew Halloway Playhouse is flying a scenic backdrop cloth for tonight's single performance of the touring production, a fixed one-show flying plot with no substitute backdrop available in the venue's stock.

The flying bar assigned to this cloth is rated for a maximum line-set load of 80 kilograms across its counterweight system. The backdrop cloth, battened and dressed, weighs a measured 105 kilograms. The venue's rigging log states flying a load above the rated line-set capacity is not permitted.

The stage's grid requires a minimum clearance of 9 metres above the proscenium arch for the cloth to fly out fully during scene changes. This venue's grid height above the proscenium measures only 7.2 metres. The venue's technical specification states a flying piece cannot clear the grid if the piece's full-out height exceeds available clearance.

Venue fire code requires the fire curtain's interlock system to show a ready-and-tested status before any scenic piece may fly across the proscenium line during a performance. Tonight's pre-show test logs the interlock status as fault, following an unresolved sensor fault from the matinee. Fire code states flying across the proscenium line with a faulted interlock is prohibited.

correction.text: Rigging update: with additional counterweight added to the arbor, the flying bar's rated line-set capacity is now 115 kilograms, replacing the 80-kilogram figure.

presented.question: Considering the flying bar's rated line-set capacity, the grid's minimum clearance above the proscenium, and the fire-curtain interlock's readiness rule, can the backdrop flying cue proceed as planned, and if not what stops it?

claims.text: Document 4 corrects document 1's statement that the flying bar's rated line-set capacity is 80 kilograms, updating it to 115 kilograms after additional counterweight was added.
The backdrop cloth weighs a measured 105 kilograms.
With the corrected capacity of 115 kilograms, the 105-kilogram backdrop cloth is now within the rated line-set load, so the weight limit no longer prevents flying the cloth.
The stage's grid requires a minimum clearance of 9 metres above the proscenium arch for the cloth to fly out fully.
This venue's grid height above the proscenium measures only 7.2 metres, which is less than the required 9 metres.

The venue's technical specification states a flying piece cannot clear the grid if the piece's full-out height exceeds available clearance, so the cloth cannot fly out fully due to insufficient grid clearance.
Fire code requires the fire curtain's interlock system to show a ready-and-tested status before any scenic piece may fly across the proscenium line during a performance.
Tonight's pre-show test logged the interlock status as fault, due to an unresolved sensor fault from the matinee.
Fire code prohibits flying across the proscenium line with a faulted interlock, so the fire-curtain interlock fault independently blocks the flying cue.

The venue has a fixed one-show flying plot for tonight's single performance with no substitute backdrop available in stock, meaning the cloth cannot be swapped for an alternative that might avoid the clearance or interlock issues.

model_output.conclusion: The backdrop flying cue cannot proceed as planned: although the updated 115-kilogram line-set capacity now safely accommodates the 105-kilogram cloth, the cue is still blocked because the grid's 7.2-metre clearance is insufficient for the required 9-metre full-out height and, independently, the fire curtain's interlock is in a faulted (not ready-and-tested) state, which fire code prohibits for any flying across the proscenium line.

presented.must_persist_fact: The flown piece is a scenic backdrop cloth for tonight's single performance.
